mnemonics.title: Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU)
mnemonics.content: Rectified – negatives become zero; Linear – straight line for x>0; Unit – a simple activation function.

mnemonics.title: ReLU Rules Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: R = Rectify (reject negatives), E = Excite (pass positives), L = Linear (slope 1), U = Unit (output unchanged).

mnemonics.title: Softmax – Soft Maximum
mnemonics.content: Softmax = Soft + Max; it turns raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1, amplifying the biggest score but keeping the distribution smooth.

mnemonics.title: Softmax Probability Calculation
mnemonics.content: Take each logit, compute e^logit, then divide each by the sum of all e^logits; the result is a probability distribution.

mnemonics.title: ReLU vs Sigmoid
mnemonics.content: ReLU is a ramp (straight up), Sigmoid is an S‑curve; ReLU helps avoid vanishing gradients, Sigmoid squashes values between 0 and 1.

acronyms.usage: Applied in hidden layers to introduce non‑linearity while keeping computation cheap; it outputs 0 for negative inputs and the identity for positive inputs.
Used in the output layer for multi‑class classification to convert logits into a probability distribution that sums to 1.

acronyms.expansion: Raw (unnormalized) scores from the previous layer
acronyms.usage: These are the inputs to the Softmax function; they can be any real numbers and are exponentiated during Softmax conversion.

acronyms.expansion: Function that determines a neuron’s output given its input
acronyms.usage: Introduces non‑linearity into the network; ReLU and Softmax are common activation functions.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a ramp starting at the origin (0,0) that goes upward to the right; the left side is flat at y=0.
Think of a light switch: off (0) for x<0, on (1) for x>0; the slope changes abruptly at 0.
Picture a bar chart where each class is a bar; the height of each bar is its probability, and the bars together fill the entire height (sum = 1).
Hardmax selects the tallest bar and makes it 1, all others 0; Softmax gives each bar a smooth, proportional height based on exponentiated scores.
Imagine a tiny hill (small logit) becoming a mountain after applying e^x; a slight difference in logits leads to a large difference in probabilities.

analogies.analogy: A club bouncer checks IDs: anyone younger than 18 (negative) is turned away (output 0), adults (positive) walk in unchanged.
In a shareholder meeting each shareholder (class) gets votes proportional to their shares; the total votes always equal 100%.
Think of water in a pipe: positive pressure (positive input) pushes water forward (gradient passes), negative pressure blocks flow (gradient stops).
Imagine several buckets of water poured into a larger container, then redistributed so each bucket ends up with a share of the total.
A straight road: as you accelerate (positive input), your speed (output) increases at a constant rate; negatives are like roadblocks that stop you.
